description.en: Turn responsible AI from slideware into controls: Microsoft's RAI principles mapped to Foundry features, Azure Policy guardrails, model inventories and audit trails, human oversight, and readiness for regulation like the EU AI Act.

story_variants.en:
## Governance Is What Survives the Demo

Every capability in this track — deployments, agents, RAG, evaluations, safety filters, tracing, cost controls — answers a technical question. Governance answers the organizational one: *how does a company let hundreds of people build with AI without any one of them being able to create a catastrophe?* Done badly, governance is a committee that says no slowly. Done well, it is **paved roads**: platform defaults that make the safe path the easy path, with evidence generated automatically as teams build.

> The goal is not zero risk — it is *known, owned, and bounded* risk, with a paper trail that satisfies auditors and, increasingly, regulators.

## Principles to Controls: The Mapping Discipline

Microsoft's Responsible AI standard names six principles: **fairness, reliability & safety, privacy & security, inclusiveness, transparency, accountability.** Principles do not ship; controls do. The core governance skill is mapping each principle to concrete Foundry mechanisms:

| Principle | Concrete control in Foundry/Azure |
|---|---|
| Reliability & safety | Evaluation gates in CI (lesson 7), content filter configs (lesson 8), red-team cadence (lesson 9) |
| Privacy & security | Hub network isolation, managed identity, connection-vaulted secrets, security-trimmed RAG (lessons 1, 5, 10) |
| Transparency | Model cards from the catalog, citation-bearing answers, user disclosure that AI is in the loop |
| Accountability | RBAC with named owners, audit logs, traces retained per policy (lesson 13) |
| Fairness | Evaluation slices across user groups; red-team probes for disparate behavior |
| Inclusiveness | Accessibility review; language and reading-level requirements in prompts and evals |

If a principle in your policy document maps to no control, it is decoration. If a control maps to no principle, ask why you pay for it.

## The Governance Stack in Azure Terms

- **Azure Policy** — declarative guardrails evaluated by the platform: allowed model families, allowed regions, required network isolation, required content-filter baselines. Policy turns 'please do not' into 'cannot'.
- **RBAC (Entra ID)** — who can create hubs, projects, deployments; who can change filter configurations (that permission should be rare and audited).
- **Model inventory** — a living register of every model in use: version, owner, use case, risk tier, evaluation evidence, retirement date. Foundry's project structure plus tags gets you most of the way; the discipline is keeping it complete.
- **Audit trails** — Azure activity logs (who changed what resource), plus application traces (what the system did per request). Retention set by policy, not by default.
- **Gateway patterns** — many enterprises front all model traffic with API management (an 'AI gateway') for centralized authentication, rate limiting, logging, and model allow-lists across providers.

## Risk-Tiered Review: Not All AI Is Equal

A single review process for everything either rubber-stamps the dangerous or strangles the trivial. Mature programs tier use cases:

- **Low risk** (internal summarization, code assistance): self-service on the paved road; automated checks only.
- **Medium risk** (customer-facing content with human review): lightweight review — evidence of evaluations, safety config, an accountable owner.
- **High risk** (decisions affecting individuals: lending, hiring, health): full review — impact assessment, human-in-the-loop design, fairness slicing, ongoing monitoring commitments, sign-off.

This tiering aligns naturally with the **EU AI Act's** risk-based structure (unacceptable/high/limited/minimal, with obligations phased in from 2025 onward) and with **ISO/IEC 42001** (AI management systems) — build the tiering once, and regulatory mapping becomes bookkeeping rather than a scramble.

## Human Oversight That Actually Oversees

'A human reviews the output' fails when the human rubber-stamps 400 items a day. Design oversight like an engineer:

- **Gate by consequence**: automated for low-stakes, human approval above thresholds (the refund cap from lesson 11 was governance in miniature).
- **Make review informative**: show the reviewer the citations, safety scores, and trace — not just the answer.
- **Measure the overseers**: approval rates near 100% with sub-second decisions means the control has decayed into theater; sample-audit the reviews.
- **Design escalation paths**: users and employees need a way to contest AI-influenced outcomes, and those reports must flow into the incident process.

## Incidents: When (Not If) Something Goes Wrong

AI incidents — harmful output that reached a user, a data leak through a prompt, an agent taking a wrong action — need the same machinery as security incidents: severity definitions, an on-call owner, comms templates, and post-incident reviews whose action items land in the eval suite (lesson 7), the filter config (lesson 8), and the red-team plan (lesson 9). The track's recurring loop appears one final time: **every failure becomes a permanent test.**

## Pitfalls

- **Governance by document.** A 40-page policy nobody operationalized is risk *transfer* to the auditor's finding, not risk management.
- **The central bottleneck.** If every use case queues for one review board, teams route around governance entirely — shadow AI is born from slow yes.
- **Point-in-time compliance.** Approved-at-launch means little after four model updates; controls must be continuous (policy evaluation, online monitoring, scheduled re-red-teaming).
- **Ignoring shadow AI.** Employees are already pasting data into consumer chatbots; a governed, capable internal platform is the *carrot* that makes rules followable.
- **No inventory.** You cannot answer a regulator's 'which systems use model X?' if you never kept the register. Start it now, while the list is short.

## Key Takeaways — and the Track's Closing Argument

Governance maps principles to controls: Azure Policy and RBAC as guardrails, model inventories and audit trails as evidence, risk-tiered review as proportionality, engineered human oversight, and incident loops that feed the evaluation suite. Everything you built in this track *is* the governance program: measured quality, layered safety, adversarial testing, observability, and cost accountability. Responsible AI is not a final chapter bolted onto engineering — it is what this whole track has been teaching, assembled.

description.en: Principles become controls; controls generate evidence; incidents feed back into the register and the tests.

left.label: A 40-page policy PDF and a monthly review board queue

right.label: Paved roads: policy-enforced defaults, tiered review, automatic evidence

description.en: Governance that works makes the safe path the easy path — slow yes breeds shadow AI.
The lifecycle of a governed AI use case, from intake to continuous assurance.

explanation.en: The control baseline is data — reviewable, versionable, testable.
Compliance checks return the GAPS, not just a boolean; findings must be actionable.
Sweeping the whole inventory on a schedule is what turns point-in-time approval into continuous assurance.

lines: prod-bot         PASS  (owner: team-support, tier: medium, evals: 2026-06-28)
-> ticket GOV-114 filed, owner: team-legacy, SLA: 14 days

description.en: A governance sweep produces findings with owners and deadlines — evidence and enforcement in one pass.

instruction.en: Automate a governance compliance sweep. REQUIRED lists three policy keys: 'content_filter', 'managed_identity', 'network_isolation'. Implement check(deployment) returning a list of the REQUIRED keys whose value in the deployment dict is falsy or absent (in REQUIRED order). For each starter deployment, in order, print '<name>: PASS' if the list is empty, else '<name>: FAIL (missing: <keys joined by comma-space>)'. Finally print 'Compliance: <passing>/<total>'. This is the continuous-assurance sweep from the lesson, in miniature.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
REQUIRED = ['content_filter', 'managed_identity', 'network_isolation']

def check(deployment):
    # TODO: return list of REQUIRED keys that are missing or falsy, in REQUIRED order
    pass

deployments = [
    {'name': 'prod-bot', 'content_filter': True, 'managed_identity': True, 'network_isolation': True},
    {'name': 'legacy-tool', 'content_filter': False, 'managed_identity': False, 'network_isolation': True},
]

# TODO: print per-deployment PASS/FAIL lines, then f'Compliance: {passing}/{len(deployments)}'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
prod-bot: PASS
legacy-tool: FAIL (missing: content_filter, managed_identity)
Compliance: 1/2

en: check: return [k for k in REQUIRED if not deployment.get(k)] — .get() handles absent keys, 'not' handles False.
Iterating REQUIRED (not the deployment dict) guarantees the missing keys print in policy order.
Join the gaps with ', ' and count passes with a counter incremented when the missing list is empty.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert check({'content_filter': True, 'managed_identity': True, 'network_isolation': True}) == []
assert check({'content_filter': False, 'managed_identity': False, 'network_isolation': True}) == ['content_filter', 'managed_identity']
assert check({}) == ['content_filter', 'managed_identity', 'network_isolation']
assert check({'content_filter': True}) == ['managed_identity', 'network_isolation']

en: Blocking all AI use until reviewed
Platform defaults that make the safe path the easiest path, with evidence generated automatically

q.en: Which Azure mechanism turns 'teams should not deploy unapproved model families' into an enforced rule?
Why tier AI use cases by risk instead of one review process for all?

en: Uniform review either rubber-stamps dangerous cases or strangles trivial ones; tiering matches scrutiny to consequence (and aligns with the EU AI Act)
Only high-risk systems can use GPT models

q.en: A human reviewer approves 100% of AI outputs at sub-second speed. What does this signal?

en: Excellent model quality, oversight can be removed
The oversight control has decayed into theater and needs redesign and sample-auditing
